message.content: Here is a launch‑checklist of **image and media performance optimizations** that are explicitly covered by **web.dev** and **Lighthouse**, with the **exact Lighthouse audit IDs** and where web.dev documents the practice. --- ## 1. Modern image formats (WebP / AVIF) **Goal:** Serve images in **modern formats** (WebP, AVIF) instead of legacy formats (JPEG/PNG) when supported. - **Lighthouse audit ID:** - **`modern-image-formats`** (UI text: “Serve images in modern formats”). - Historically also exposed as **`uses-webp-images`** in some UIs; the canonical current audit ID in Lighthouse is `modern-image-formats`.[1] - **What Lighthouse checks:** Images that could be significantly smaller if served as WebP/AVIF and estimates potential byte savings.[1] - **Where web.dev documents it:** - “Optimizing Web Vitals using Lighthouse” notes that *“Lighthouse includes a number of image optimization audits for helping you understand if your images could be better compressed, resized or delivered in a more optimal modern image format.”*[1] **Launch check:** - Use **WebP** and **AVIF** where browser support is sufficient, with fallbacks if necessary. - Ensure the **LCP image** is also provided in a modern format when possible. --- ## 2. Responsive images (`srcset` / `sizes`) **Goal:** Avoid sending unnecessarily large images to small viewports; serve device‑appropriate resolutions. - **Lighthouse audit ID:** - **`uses-responsive-images`** (UI text: “Serve images with appropriate size / properly size images for different devices”). - **What Lighthouse checks:** Compares rendered image dimensions to the intrinsic resource size and flags images that are much larger than needed, often due to missing or suboptimal `srcset`/`sizes`.[1][3] - **Where web.dev documents it:** - web.dev articles on responsive images and the Lighthouse optimization article reference image resizing and right‑sizing as key checks.[1] **Launch check:** - Use `srcset` and `sizes` for all content images that can appear at different sizes on different viewports. - Ensure that the **displayed CSS size** roughly matches the **selected resource size** at common breakpoints. --- ## 3. Explicit width/height to avoid layout shift (images & video) **Goal:** Prevent **Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)** caused by images and videos that do not reserve space before loading. - **Lighthouse audit ID:** - **`unsized-images`** (UI text: “Image elements do not have explicit width and height”). - **What Lighthouse checks:** Identifies `` elements without width/height (or equivalent CSS aspect ratio) that may cause layout shifts.[1] - **Where web.dev documents it:** - web.dev explicitly states: *“To limit Cumulative Layout Shift being caused by images without dimensions, include width and height size attributes on your images and video elements.”*[1] **Launch check:** - For **all images and video elements**, set: - HTML `width` and `height` attributes that reflect the intrinsic aspect ratio, or - CSS that defines an **aspect-ratio** (via the `aspect-ratio` property or fixed dimensions). - Verify in Lighthouse that the **`unsized-images`** audit passes and that CLS is low. --- ## 4. Lazy‑loading offscreen images **Goal:** Defer loading of images that are not in or near the initial viewport to reduce initial load and improve **LCP** and **Total Byte Weight**. - **Lighthouse audit ID:** - **`offscreen-images`** (UI text: “Defer offscreen images”). - **What Lighthouse checks:** Detects images that are not visible above the fold and are not marked for lazy‑loading, estimating potential savings.[1][3] - **Where web.dev documents it:** - web.dev’s Lighthouse + Web Vitals article notes that Lighthouse “includes a number of image optimization audits” for compression, resizing, and modern formats and references lazy‑loading patterns when discussing widgets/facades and LCP image behavior.[1] **Launch check:** - Use `loading="lazy"` (or an equivalent intersection‑observer based lazy‑loader) for **non‑critical, below‑the‑fold** images. - Ensure the **LCP image is not lazily loaded** (see section 7). --- ## 5. Properly sized / avoiding oversized images **Goal:** Avoid **oversized images** (e.g., 3000px image displayed at 300px) to reduce bytes and speed up rendering. - **Lighthouse audit IDs (two related checks):** 1. **`uses-responsive-images`** - Flags images that are much larger than their display size, which often implies missing or incorrect responsive image markup. 2. **`offscreen-images`** (indirect) - While primarily for lazy‑loading, its recommendations often highlight cases where large offscreen images are loaded unnecessarily. - **Where web.dev documents it:** - The Web Vitals optimization article notes Lighthouse “image optimization audits for helping you understand if your images could be better compressed, resized…”[1] - Performance Insights documentation (DebugBear article summarizing Lighthouse insights) specifically notes an insight for **“Serving images at the right size”** and “Increasing the compression factor.”[3] **Launch check:** - Ensure that: - The **intrinsic image resolution** is not dramatically larger than the maximum rendered size for that context. - `srcset` provides multiple resolutions and **browser picks** a resource close to the actual required size. - Re‑run Lighthouse to ensure **no significant savings** are reported under image sizing opportunities. --- ## 6. Efficiently encoded / optimized images **Goal:** Ensure images are compressed efficiently (quality vs size) to reduce transfer size without visible quality loss. - **Lighthouse audit ID:** - **`uses-optimized-images`** (UI text: “Efficiently encode images”). - **What Lighthouse checks:** Analyzes image compression; reports potential byte savings if images were encoded more efficiently (e.g., more aggressive JPEG/WebP compression).[1][3] - **Where web.dev documents it:** - The Web Vitals + Lighthouse article mentions “image optimization audits” for images that “could be better compressed.”[1] - The Performance Insights documentation (DebugBear) shows an insight that “highlights a few potential optimizations to improve image delivery, for example: Serving images at the right size; Increasing the compression factor.”[3] **Launch check:** - Re‑encode large images with modern encoders (e.g., mozjpeg, WebP, AVIF encoders) and quality tuned until Lighthouse no longer shows large potential savings in **`uses-optimized-images`**. --- ## 7. Preloading the LCP image & avoiding incorrect lazy‑loading **Goal:** Make the **Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) image** load as early and as quickly as possible. - **Related Lighthouse audits / checks:** 1. **`lcp-lazy-loaded`** (UI text: “Largest Contentful Paint image was lazily loaded”). - Highlights when the element identified as the LCP image uses lazy‑loading, which delays LCP.[1] 2. **LCP request discovery audit** (Performance Insights, experimental) - DebugBear’s summary of Lighthouse’s Performance Insights describes an “LCP request discovery audit” that checks that “the LCP image request happens as soon as possible.”[3] 3. **“Largest Contentful Paint element”** audit (diagnostic, not an opportunity). - Helps identify which element is LCP so you can optimize it.[1] - **Where web.dev documents it:** - web.dev: *“Lighthouse has a ‘Largest Contentful Paint element’ audit that identifies what element was the largest contentful paint.”*[1] - *“Lighthouse will highlight if an LCP image is being incorrectly lazy-loaded via the ‘Largest Contentful Paint image was lazily loaded’ audit.”*[1] - The article recommends preloading the LCP image when appropriate as part of optimizing LCP timing.[1] **Launch check:** - Ensure: - The **LCP element** is identified via the “Largest Contentful Paint element” diagnostic in Lighthouse.[1] - The corresponding image is **not lazy‑loaded** (do not use `loading="lazy"` for it).[1] - Use `` for the LCP image when it is known at page load to start fetching it earlier. --- ## 8. Image CDN / efficient delivery **Goal:** Deliver images from a **fast, optimized CDN** with on‑the‑fly resizing/compression when possible. - **Lighthouse audits:** There is no single canonical “image CDN” audit ID, but image‑related audits (e.g., `uses-responsive-images`, `uses-optimized-images`, `offscreen-images`, `modern-image-formats`) will indirectly surface opportunities often solved by using an image CDN.[1][3] - **Where web.dev documents it:** - The Web Vitals + Lighthouse article refers to image optimization in terms of resizing, compression, and modern formats, which are common features of image CDNs.[1] **Launch check:** - Use an **image CDN** that supports: - Automatic format negotiation (WebP/AVIF), - Dynamic resizing and DPR-aware variants, - Aggressive caching and HTTP/2/3. - Confirm via Lighthouse that key image audits report minimal remaining savings. --- ## 9. Animated content efficiency (animated GIF vs video) **Goal:** Avoid heavy animated GIFs; use video/WebP/AVIF animation for better performance. - **Lighthouse audit ID:** - **`efficient-animated-content`** (UI text: “Use video formats for animated content”). - **What Lighthouse checks:** Detects large animated GIFs and suggests using video formats instead for better compression and streaming behavior. - **Where web.dev documents it:** - web.dev and Lighthouse documentation for performance audits include an “Efficiently animate images” / “Use video formats for animated content” audit as part of image optimization guidance, summarized as using **`efficient-animated-content`**.[1] **Launch check:** - Replace large animated GIFs with: - `
